topic: [sociology/long/gene_genome] Examine how remote work reshapes social class boundaries, workplace culture, and access to career advancement. Compare experiences across workers with different
full_prompt: Examine how remote work reshapes social class boundaries, workplace culture, and access to career advancement. Compare experiences across workers with different levels of income, education, and household responsibilities.
completion:
I’ll begin by mapping the empirical sociology and adjacent labor literature on remote-work stratification, looking for studies that connect telework access to class, gender/household labor, and advancement rather than treating remote work as a uniform benefit.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "remote work telework inequality social class career advancement household responsibilities sociology", "year_min": 2015, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: Search returned candidates including Lyttelton, Zang & Musick 2022 on gender differences in telecommuting (`https://openalex.org/W4282575511`), Felstead & Henseke 2017 on remote work and organizational culture (`https://openalex.org/W2737227217`), Dingel & Neiman 2020 on feasibility of work from home by occupation (`https://openalex.org/W3035865980`), and Chung et al. 2021 on flexible working and inequalities (`https://openalex.org/W3208572268`). These suggest a useful split between “who can work remotely,” “what remote work does to workplace culture,” and “how remote work interacts with family obligations.” 

I’ll read the occupation-level feasibility paper first because it anchors class boundaries: remote work is not merely chosen by workers but allocated through occupational structure, credentials, and income.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W3035865980"}
```  
[result]: Dingel & Neiman estimate that roughly 37% of U.S. jobs can plausibly be performed at home, with feasibility concentrated in high-wage, high-education occupations. The paper provides a structural baseline for classed access but says less about within-remote variation in workplace culture or advancement once workers are remote. 

Next I’ll read the household-responsibilities study to understand how remote work is experienced differently by gender and caregiving status, especially among comparatively privileged workers who have access to telework.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W4282575511"}
```  
[result]: Lyttelton, Zang & Musick show that telecommuting during the pandemic often intensified unpaid domestic work for mothers and did not automatically reduce gender inequality at home. The study highlights that remote-work “flexibility” can become a mechanism for absorbing caregiving shocks, making household composition a key source of unequal career consequences. 

I’ll now examine organizational culture and boundary control, since career advancement depends not only on job feasibility but also on visibility, trust, and informal access inside workplaces.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W2737227217"}
```  
[result]: Felstead & Henseke find that remote working is associated with autonomy and work intensification, but also with altered relationships to managers and colleagues. Their evidence points to a paradox: remote work can increase control over time while weakening access to informal workplace culture and recognition. 

To sharpen the advancement mechanism, I’ll search for literature on telework, visibility penalties, promotion, and “ideal worker” norms, including hybrid-work studies that may reveal how remote workers are compared to office-present peers.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "telework remote work visibility promotion career advancement ideal worker norm proximity bias", "year_min": 2000, "year_max": 2025, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: Search surfaced Bloom et al. 2015 field experiment on working from home (`https://openalex.org/W2107026047`), Leslie et al. 2012 on flexible work practices and career premiums/penalties (`https://openalex.org/W2164115052`), Kelly et al. 2014 on workplace flexibility and work-family conflict (`https://openalex.org/W2144935144`), and Golden & Eddleston 2020 on remote work and promotion/salary growth (`https://openalex.org/W3002340719`). The results indicate that career consequences vary depending on whether remote work signals commitment, accommodation need, or high-status autonomy. 

I’ll read the experimental evidence because it separates productivity effects from career-perception effects, a distinction that is central to studying advancement boundaries.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W2107026047"}
```  
[result]: Bloom et al. find that home working in a Chinese call-center experiment increased performance and reduced attrition, but promotion rates were lower among home workers, partly because they were less visible. This offers a concrete mechanism: remote work can produce productivity gains while simultaneously weakening advancement through reduced managerial attention. 

I’ll read the promotion/salary paper to see whether advancement penalties are universal or contingent on extent of remote work, face time, and organizational norms.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W3002340719"}
```  
[result]: Golden & Eddleston report that telework’s effects on salary growth and promotion depend on work context, with high telework intensity carrying penalties unless accompanied by supplemental face-to-face contact or strong organizational support. This supports a relational rather than purely individual explanation: remote work changes the currency of trust, sponsorship, and visibility. 

I’ll extract mechanism-style “genomes” from the key papers to identify what each explains and what remains underdeveloped for a sociological proposal.  
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W3035865980"}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = occupational feasibility sorting; niche = labor-market stratification during pandemic/remote-work expansion; observation = high-income, high-education workers disproportionately occupy jobs doable from home; limitation = measures access to remote-capable jobs rather than lived workplace culture or advancement; claim = remote work reproduces class inequality by mapping onto occupational and credential hierarchies. 

I’ll extract a second genome from the household-responsibilities paper to capture the domestic-side mechanism that an occupational feasibility model misses.  
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W4282575511"}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = gendered caregiving absorption under telework; niche = dual-earner and parent households during COVID-era remote work; observation = remote work increases schedule flexibility but often increases mothers’ unpaid labor and interruptions; limitation = focuses primarily on gendered household labor, not classed differences in organizational sponsorship and advancement; claim = telework may intensify rather than resolve inequalities when domestic responsibilities are unequally distributed. 

I’ll extract from the promotion/visibility study to isolate the organizational-side mechanism that connects remote arrangements to career outcomes.  
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W3002340719"}
```  
[result]: Extracted genome: mechanism = visibility-contingent advancement; niche = teleworkers in organizations where promotion depends on managerial recognition and face-time norms; observation = high-intensity telework can reduce promotion/salary growth unless offset by face-to-face contact or supportive cultures; limitation = less attention to how income, education, and household constraints shape workers’ ability to perform compensatory visibility; claim = remote work carries career penalties when it disrupts informal recognition systems. 

The emerging gap is not simply that remote work is unequal, but that different groups must pay different “visibility costs” to convert flexibility into advancement; I’ll check whether this mechanism has been explicitly framed as a classed visibility bargain.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "remote work as a classed visibility bargain in which workers trade spatial flexibility for access to sponsorship, informal culture, and promotion, with the tradeoff moderated by income, education, and household responsibilities", "year_min": 2015, "year_max": 2025}
```  
[result]: Novelty check found related literatures on telework access, flexibility stigma, proximity bias, gendered caregiving, and occupational inequality, but few studies integrate all three levels: classed eligibility for remote work, unequal capacity to maintain visibility, and household-mediated advancement tradeoffs. The proposed framing appears moderately novel as a synthetic sociological mechanism rather than a wholly new empirical domain. 

I’ll compare a draft proposed genome against the strongest parent paper to clarify the incremental contribution beyond occupational access models.  
```action
{"tool": "genome_diff", "parent_id": "https://openalex.org/W3035865980", "proposed_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "classed visibility bargain: remote work reclassifies employees by both access to remote-capable jobs and capacity to remain visible, culturally included, and sponsorable while away from the office", "niche_genome": "post-pandemic hybrid and remote workers stratified by income, education, occupational autonomy, and household caregiving responsibilities", "observation_genome": "high-income workers are more likely to access remote work, but lower-autonomy remote workers and caregivers face greater interruption, surveillance, and reduced informal sponsorship", "limitation_genome": "prior feasibility studies identify who can work from home but not how remote work reshapes intra-organizational class boundaries after access is granted", "delta_genome": "move from binary remote-feasible occupation to multilevel analysis of access, cultural belonging, and promotion conversion", "claim_genome": "remote work redraws class boundaries by making advancement depend on workers’ resources for producing visibility and availability across home and workplace domains"}}
```  
[result]: Diff indicates that the proposed genome extends Dingel & Neiman by shifting from occupational feasibility to post-access stratification. It adds mechanisms of cultural inclusion, sponsorship, household interruption, and autonomy, while preserving the insight that remote-work access is rooted in classed occupational structure. 

I’ll now propose the integrated sociological research genome, centered on remote work as a mechanism that both reflects existing class inequalities and creates new advancement boundaries through visibility, culture, and household capacity.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Remote work operates as a classed visibility bargain: workers gain spatial flexibility only insofar as they can preserve managerial trust, informal cultural inclusion, sponsorship, and signals of availability while physically absent from the workplace.", "niche_genome": "Post-pandemic remote and hybrid workers across income, education, occupational autonomy, and household-responsibility gradients, especially comparing high-income professionals, lower-autonomy white-collar workers, and caregivers in households with unequal domestic demands.", "observation_genome": "Remote-work access is concentrated among higher-income and higher-education workers, but access alone does not guarantee advantage; workers with fewer resources, less autonomy, weaker networks, or heavier caregiving responsibilities face more surveillance, interruption, isolation from workplace culture, and reduced access to informal advancement channels.", "limitation_genome": "Prior research often separates three processes: occupational feasibility of working from home, gendered household consequences of flexibility, and promotion penalties from reduced visibility. Less work integrates these into a multilevel account of how remote work redraws social class boundaries inside organizations after remote access is granted.", "delta_genome": "Shift the analytic focus from a binary question of who can work from home to a stratified process of who can convert remote work into career advancement, examining how income, education, household labor, occupational autonomy, and workplace culture jointly determine the costs of staying visible.", "claim_genome": "Remote work reshapes class boundaries by making career advancement depend less on physical presence alone and more on unequal capacities to manufacture visibility, availability, and belonging across home and workplace domains; consequently, remote work can simultaneously protect privileged workers, discipline less-autonomous workers, and intensify penalties for caregivers."}}
```

topic: [sociology/short/gene_genome] Investigate how cultural representations of poverty influence public attitudes toward welfare policy. Compare portrayals in news media, entertainment, and polit

completion:
I’ll do one targeted literature search to anchor the trajectory in sociology/political communication work that directly links poverty portrayals to welfare attitudes across media arenas.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "cultural representations poverty news entertainment political discourse public attitudes welfare policy", "year_min": 1990, "year_max": 2024, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: Candidate papers included Gilens, “Race and Poverty in America” (OpenAlex: W2049200707), Iyengar, “Framing Responsibility for Political Issues” (W2147310690), Clawson & Trice, “Poverty as We Know It” (W2016466847), Bullock et al., “Media Images of the Poor” (W2102650457), and Rose & Baumgartner, “Framing the Poor” (W1977495345). The strongest common thread is that episodic, racialized, and moralized depictions shift blame toward individuals and reduce support for redistributive welfare.  

I’ll read the Rose & Baumgartner article because it offers a longer-run framing account of media coverage and poverty policy, useful for integrating news with political discourse.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W1977495345"}
```  
[result]: Rose & Baumgartner argue that poverty coverage is politically consequential because media frames fluctuate between structural/economic explanations and behavioral/individual-failing explanations. Their evidence suggests policy responsiveness is constrained when poverty is represented through disorder, dependency, or undeservingness rather than labor-market insecurity and institutional exclusion.

The results suggest a gap in connecting everyday teen fashion/tech consumption with algorithmically amplified influencer norms and peer-status negotiation.
I now propose a short sociological study that treats teenage consumer identity as produced across offline peer groups and online influencer cultures, rather than as simple individual choice.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "This project examines how consumer culture shapes identity formation among teenagers by focusing on five linked domains: fashion, personal technology, branding, peer status, and digital influencers. The central research question is: how do teenagers use consumer goods and branded styles to construct, display, and negotiate who they are among peers? Rather than treating consumption as superficial, the study approaches clothes, phones, sneakers, beauty products, gaming devices, and platform aesthetics as symbolic resources through which young people perform belonging, distinction, aspiration, and resistance.\n\nThe study will use a mixed qualitative design combining semi-structured interviews, small peer-group discussions, and digital ethnography. Interviews with teenagers aged 13–18 will explore how they interpret brands, trends, influencer recommendations, and pressure to own particular items. Peer-group discussions will examine how status judgments are collectively made: what counts as “cool,” “basic,” “fake,” “rich,” “try-hard,” or “authentic.” Digital ethnography on platforms such as TikTok, Instagram, and YouTube will analyze how influencers model desirable lifestyles and how teenagers translate these models into everyday choices.\n\nAnalytically, the project will connect consumer culture to identity through three mechanisms. First, fashion and technology act as visible markers of taste, class position, gender performance, and group membership. Second, branding provides ready-made identity scripts, allowing teenagers to associate themselves with values such as luxury, creativity, athleticism, environmental awareness, or rebellion. Third, digital influencers intensify consumer identity work by making trends more immediate, personalized, and emotionally persuasive, while also linking consumption to metrics of visibility such as likes, comments, shares, and follower counts.\n\nThe expected contribution is a sociological account of teenage identity as a status negotiation shaped by both market culture and peer surveillance. The project will show how teenagers are not passive consumers, but active interpreters who adopt, remix, reject, or parody branded meanings. At the same time, it will examine inequalities in consumer identity: not all teenagers have equal access to fashionable goods or technologies, and lack of access can become a source of exclusion, embarrassment, or creative adaptation. The study will therefore contribute to debates on youth culture, digital capitalism, symbolic boundaries, and the commercialization of everyday selfhood."}
```

[result]: Manville finds that Los Angeles’s adaptive reuse ordinance, which relaxed parking requirements for older downtown buildings, enabled many residential conversions that would otherwise have been infeasible. Developers often supplied less parking than conventional minimums required, lowering project costs and supporting housing production, though the paper is less direct on post-occupancy car ownership and curb congestion.

Next I read a complementary paper on whether required/provided residential parking changes car ownership, since the topic asks about behavioral and congestion channels beyond development feasibility.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2097161175"}

[result]: Guo’s New York City study shows that off-street residential parking supply is positively associated with household vehicle ownership even in transit-rich neighborhoods, suggesting parking reform may reduce car ownership by reducing bundled supply. It also warns that spillover parking and curb management matter: lower off-street supply does not automatically reduce street congestion unless curb space is priced or regulated.

I extract the conceptual “genome” of the Los Angeles paper to identify what is already established and where a new research contribution could add value.

However, few peer-reviewed studies jointly estimate cost pass-through, car ownership adaptation, curb occupancy/congestion, and affordability outcomes with comparable treated and control neighborhoods.

The resulting proposal extends existing reform studies by combining permit/building-cost data, household vehicle registration, curb occupancy, and rent/sales outcomes across cities that reduced or eliminated minimums in dense neighborhoods.

completion:
I’ll first look for empirical work connecting working memory capacity/load to risky choice, because that anchors the mechanism before adding time pressure and confidence calibration.  
```action

```  
[result]: Found candidate papers including Hinson et al. “Impulsive decision making and working memory” (OpenAlex: W2034756711), Whitney et al. “Framing effects under cognitive load” (OpenAlex: W2049247767), Pleskac & Busemeyer on dynamic signal detection/confidence (OpenAlex: W2133698169), and time-pressure decision studies in preferential choice. Hinson et al. is the strongest direct parent for WM-load effects on risky/impulsive choice.  

I’ll read the core working-memory/risky-choice paper to extract the causal handle and limitations for a follow-up design.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2034756711"}
```  
[result]: Hinson et al. report that taxing working memory during decision tasks increases preference for disadvantageous/immediate options, consistent with WM supporting integration of past outcomes and inhibition of tempting risky choices. The work is informative mechanistically but does not cleanly separate trait WMC from experimentally imposed load, nor does it focus on time pressure or confidence calibration.  

Next I’ll read a decision-confidence model paper to connect speeded evidence accumulation with confidence, since the proposed study needs risk preference and calibration outcomes together.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2133698169"}
```  
[result]: Pleskac & Busemeyer’s two-stage dynamic signal-detection framework links choice, response time, and confidence through evidence accumulation and post-decisional processing. It suggests time pressure may truncate evidence accumulation and degrade confidence calibration, but it is not specifically about working-memory load or economic risk preferences.  

I’ll check whether the combined mechanism—WMC/load × time pressure jointly shifting risk preference and metacognitive calibration—has recent direct coverage.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "working memory capacity and induced cognitive load interact with time pressure to alter risky choice and confidence calibration via truncated evidence integration and reduced outcome-state maintenance", "year_min": 2015, "year_max": 2024}
```  
[result]: Recent work separately examines cognitive load and risky choice, time pressure and heuristics, and confidence under speed-accuracy tradeoffs, but few studies jointly manipulate time pressure and cognitive load while measuring individual WMC, risk-preference parameters, and trial-level confidence calibration. The niche appears viable if framed as an integrative experiment with computational modeling.  

Given the gap, I’ll propose a compact study genome emphasizing a factorial design, trait WMC measurement, risky choice modeling, and calibration analysis.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Trait working memory capacity supports maintenance and integration of probability, payoff, and recent outcome information during risky choice; acute cognitive load and time pressure jointly reduce this integration window, increasing reliance on simpler heuristics and weakening metacognitive evidence for confidence judgments.", "niche_genome": "Complex decision-making under experimentally crossed cognitive load and response-deadline pressure, with individual differences in working memory capacity used to predict changes in risk preference and confidence calibration.", "observation_genome": "In a preregistered laboratory task, participants complete operation-span/symmetry-span WMC measures, then make repeated mixed-gamble and multi-attribute risky choices under low vs high secondary-memory load and relaxed vs speeded deadlines, rating confidence after each choice. Outcomes include choice-derived risk-aversion/loss-aversion parameters, response times, confidence, calibration error, and over/underconfidence.", "limitation_genome": "Prior studies show that cognitive load can increase impulsive or disadvantageous choices and that time pressure can truncate evidence accumulation, but they often isolate these factors, use coarse risk outcomes, or omit trial-level confidence calibration and trait WMC moderation.", "delta_genome": "Combine trait WMC, induced load, and deadline pressure in one within-subject design, then fit hierarchical choice models and calibration curves to test whether load-induced risk shifts are strongest for lower-WMC individuals and whether confidence becomes miscalibrated specifically when both memory maintenance and deliberation time are constrained.", "claim_genome": "If working memory is a shared resource for risk integration and metacognitive monitoring, high cognitive load under time pressure should produce more heuristic/risk-seeking or myopically loss-avoidant choices, slower learning from feedback, and higher confidence miscalibration, with these effects attenuated among high-WMC participants."}}
```

It also notes a recurring governance problem: symbolic upgrading can privilege visitor-facing narratives over everyday resident meanings, especially when local participation is shallow.

topic: [cognitive_science/medium/idea_plan] Investigate how predictive processing models explain perceptual illusions across visual and auditory modalities. Design experiments that compare top-down expect
full_prompt: Investigate how predictive processing models explain perceptual illusions across visual and auditory modalities. Design experiments that compare top-down expectation effects under varying uncertainty.
completion:
I first search for predictive-processing work that explicitly connects expectations/priors with perception, illusions, and uncertainty, aiming to cover both visual and auditory modalities. This should identify theoretical anchors and experiment-friendly paradigms.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "predictive processing perceptual illusions visual auditory uncertainty expectations priors", "year_min": 2010, "year_max": 2024, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: Found candidates including de Lange et al. “How Do Expectations Shape Perception?” OpenAlex: `https://openalex.org/W2889317795`; Powers et al. “Pavlovian conditioning-induced hallucinations result from overweighting of perceptual priors” OpenAlex: `https://openalex.org/W2737139804`; and Teufel & Fletcher “Forms of prediction in the nervous system” OpenAlex: `https://openalex.org/W2795058962`. The strongest empirical fit is Powers et al. for auditory expectation effects; de Lange et al. provides a broad visual/perceptual framework.

I read the broad review first to extract the main predictive-processing claims about how precision/uncertainty should modulate top-down expectation effects. This helps translate the theory into cross-modal experimental manipulations.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W2889317795"}
```  
[result]: The paper distinguishes expectation effects on neural gain, template matching, and decision bias, emphasizing that expectations interact with sensory reliability/precision. It argues that perception should be most prior-driven when sensory evidence is uncertain, but that effects can appear as either perceptual sharpening or biased reports depending on task demands and measurement level.

Next I read an auditory-focused empirical paper because the proposed experiments need to compare visual and auditory illusions under a common expectation/uncertainty framework. This paper is particularly relevant because it operationalizes overweighted priors as conditioned hallucination-like percepts.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W2737139804"}
```  
[result]: Powers et al. used Pavlovian pairing of tones with visual cues to induce auditory percepts when tones were absent, showing that learned expectations can dominate perception. Their computational account links hallucination-proneness to stronger prior weighting and altered precision, suggesting an experimentally tractable way to vary expectation strength and sensory uncertainty.

I then check whether a direct cross-modal design comparing visual and auditory illusions with matched uncertainty manipulations is already common, to position the proposal as novel rather than just a replication.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "within-subject comparison of predictive-processing effects on visual and auditory illusions using matched top-down cue validity and sensory uncertainty manipulations", "year_min": 2015, "year_max": 2024}
```  
[result]: Related work exists on expectation effects within individual modalities and on hallucination-like auditory priors, but fewer studies directly match visual and auditory illusion paradigms within the same participants while parametrically varying uncertainty and cue reliability. The novelty lies in estimating a shared computational prior-weighting parameter across modalities and testing whether it generalizes or is modality-specific.

Given the literature, I propose a within-subject behavioral and computational experiment using parallel visual and auditory illusion tasks. The key manipulation is factorial: expectation strength/cue validity crossed with sensory uncertainty, allowing predictive-processing models to make quantitative cross-modal predictions.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "idea_plan": {"Idea": "Test whether predictive-processing accounts of perceptual illusions rely on a domain-general prior-weighting mechanism by comparing visual and auditory expectation-induced illusions under matched uncertainty. Participants complete parallel visual and auditory tasks in which predictive cues create top-down expectations, while stimulus reliability is manipulated; Bayesian/active-inference models estimate how much priors versus sensory evidence drive illusory perception in each modality.", "ImplementationSteps": {"1": "Design two matched illusion paradigms: a visual task using ambiguous/noisy orientation or motion stimuli preceded by predictive cues, and an auditory task using near-threshold tones or phoneme-like sounds preceded by predictive cues. In both modalities, cue validity has high, medium, and low levels so expectation strength can be separated from simple response bias.", "2": "Manipulate sensory uncertainty parametrically in each task by varying visual noise/contrast and auditory signal-to-noise ratio. Include catch trials with absent or maximally ambiguous stimuli to measure expectation-induced false percepts analogous to hallucination-like reports.", "3": "Collect within-subject behavioral data including detection/identification accuracy, confidence, reaction time, and trial-by-trial perceptual reports. Optionally add EEG/MEG to test whether expectation effects appear in early sensory responses versus later decision-related components.", "4": "Fit hierarchical Bayesian predictive-processing models in which perception is determined by cue-derived priors, sensory likelihood precision, and participant-level prior-weighting parameters. Compare models with shared cross-modal prior weighting against models with separate visual and auditory parameters.", "5": "Evaluate whether illusion magnitude increases most strongly when sensory uncertainty is high and cues are reliable, and whether individuals with strong prior weighting in vision also show strong auditory expectation effects."}, "ImplementationOrder": ["1-2", "2-3", "3-4", "4-5"], "Dataset": "New within-subject dataset from approximately 80 adult participants completing matched visual and auditory illusion tasks with factorial manipulations of cue validity and sensory uncertainty; optional EEG/MEG subset for neural validation.", "EvaluationMetrics": {"illusion_rate": "Proportion of trials on which participants report the expected percept despite ambiguous or absent sensory evidence.", "expectation_bias": "Shift in psychometric function or signal-detection criterion as a function of predictive cue validity.", "uncertainty_interaction": "Statistical interaction between cue validity and sensory uncertainty on perceptual reports, accuracy, and confidence.", "model_fit": "Out-of-sample predictive accuracy, WAIC/LOO-CV, or Bayes factors comparing shared versus modality-specific prior-weighting models.", "cross_modal_correlation": "Correlation between participant-level prior-weighting estimates in visual and auditory tasks."}, "ExpectedOutcome": "Predictive-processing theory predicts that top-down expectations will produce stronger illusions when sensory evidence is uncertain, especially under highly reliable cues. A shared-parameter model should explain part of the variance across modalities, but modality-specific parameters may also be needed, revealing whether expectation-driven illusions reflect a general predictive mechanism or sensory-domain-specific precision control."}}
```rayele

topic: [cognitive_science/medium/gene_genome] Investigate the relationship between interoceptive awareness and emotional regulation. Assess whether training attention to bodily signals improves anxiety mana
full_prompt: Investigate the relationship between interoceptive awareness and emotional regulation. Assess whether training attention to bodily signals improves anxiety management or decision quality.
completion:
I first search for recent and foundational work connecting interoception, emotion regulation, anxiety, and decision-making, prioritizing intervention studies over purely correlational accounts.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "interoceptive awareness training emotion regulation anxiety decision making heartbeat body scan mindfulness", "year_min": 2010, "year_max": 2024, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: Candidates include Garfinkel et al. on interoceptive accuracy/awareness (OpenAlex: W2143235342), Bornemann & Singer on contemplative training and heartbeat perception (OpenAlex: W2763749128), Bornemann et al. on self-reported interoceptive awareness after contemplative training (OpenAlex: W2114612926), Paulus & Stein on interoception in anxiety/depression (OpenAlex: W2069852671), and Dunn et al. on interoception and decision-making/emotion (OpenAlex: W2057297137).
I read Garfinkel et al. because it provides a useful measurement framework: bodily attention can mean accuracy, confidence, or metacognitive correspondence, and these may predict regulation differently.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2143235342"}
```  
[result]: Garfinkel et al. argue that interoception should be decomposed into objective accuracy, self-reported sensibility, and metacognitive awareness. The key implication is that “more attention to the body” is not uniformly beneficial: anxiety may involve high interoceptive sensibility without accurate or adaptive interpretation of bodily signals.
Next I read an intervention paper to assess whether training attention to bodily signals can actually change interoceptive capacities rather than merely correlate with them.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2763749128"}
```  
[result]: Bornemann & Singer report that sustained contemplative mental training, including body-focused practices, increased heartbeat perception accuracy over months and reduced alexithymia-related difficulties. The evidence supports plasticity in interoception, though benefits appear gradual and depend on practice type and measurement choice.
I extract the conceptual “genome” of the intervention evidence to identify the causal ingredients and boundary conditions for a new hypothesis.  
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "W2763749128"}
```  
[result]: Extracted pattern: mechanism = repeated low-arousal attention to internal signals improves signal discrimination and labeling; niche = contemplative/body-scan training in nonclinical adults; observation = improved heartbeat perception and emotional clarity over longitudinal practice; limitation = weak specificity for anxious arousal and little direct testing of downstream decision quality.
Given the mixed anxiety literature, I check whether the more specific idea of “calibrated” interoceptive training—attention plus accuracy feedback and emotion-labeling—has been recently framed as improving anxiety management and decision quality.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "calibrated interoceptive attention training combining bodily-signal detection, accuracy feedback, and affect labeling to improve anxiety regulation and decision quality under uncertainty", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2024}
```  
[result]: Recent work covers mindfulness/body-scan interventions, heartbeat-detection training, and interoceptive exposure for anxiety, but fewer studies integrate accuracy feedback with affect labeling and decision tasks. The proposed combination appears moderately novel, especially if tested as a mechanism linking reduced anxiety-driven misinterpretation to improved choices under risk or uncertainty.
I propose a focused study that treats interoceptive training as beneficial only when it improves calibration—accurate detection and interpretation of bodily signals—rather than merely increasing bodily vigilance.  
```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"Calibrated interoceptive attention: repeated attention to bodily signals combined with accuracy feedback and affect labeling improves discrimination between benign arousal, anxiety signals, and decision-relevant somatic cues.","niche_genome":"Adults with elevated but subclinical anxiety, especially those showing high self-reported interoceptive sensibility but low objective interoceptive accuracy or poor emotion-labeling ability.","observation_genome":"Prior work suggests interoceptive capacities are multidimensional and trainable: contemplative/body-focused practice can improve heartbeat perception and emotional clarity, while anxiety may reflect heightened bodily concern without accurate interpretation.","limitation_genome":"Existing studies often conflate bodily attention with interoceptive accuracy, rely on self-report, or test anxiety reduction without measuring whether improved bodily calibration transfers to decisions under uncertainty.","delta_genome":"Test an intervention that separates three components—bodily attention alone, bodily attention plus accuracy feedback, and bodily attention plus feedback plus affect labeling—and compare effects on anxiety recovery after stress and risky/ambiguous decision quality.","claim_genome":"Training attention to bodily signals should improve anxiety management and decision quality only when it increases interoceptive calibration; attention without calibration may amplify anxious monitoring and yield weaker or even maladaptive outcomes."}}
```

Sibling, language, and culture are classic but often siloed predictors of children’s theory-of-mind development, so I’ll first locate an integrative empirical or review anchor.

The results suggest a convergent account: social interaction quantity matters less than exposure to contrasting perspectives and explicit mental-state discourse.

I’ll read the caregiver-language anchor because it links linguistic input to mechanisms that can also explain sibling and cultural effects.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2106389473"}
```
[result]: The paper reports that caregivers’ references to desires, emotions, thoughts, and beliefs predict later theory-of-mind performance, especially when talk is explanatory and child-contingent. It supports a mechanism where language scaffolds children’s ability to represent perspectives as subjective, communicable, and sometimes conflicting.

cultures that discourage explicit belief talk may show slower performance on standard verbal false-belief tasks but not necessarily weaker practical social understanding.", "limitation_genome": "Existing findings risk conflating family size, socioeconomic status, verbal ability, task familiarity, and Western assumptions about explicit belief reasoning; sibling effects may reflect interaction quality rather than sibling presence, and caregiver-language effects may vary by culture-specific pragmatics.", "delta_genome": "Move from additive models of sibling count plus parent talk to an interactional ecology model: measure sibling conflict/cooperation, caregiver mediation language, and cultural conversational norms together, using longitudinal and culturally adapted nonverbal and narrative ToM tasks.", "claim_genome": "Children’s theory of mind develops fastest when their environment supplies both experiential evidence of divergent perspectives and culturally sanctioned language for interpreting those divergences; sibling interaction, caregiver language, and cultural norms are complementary scaffolds rather than independent predictors."}}

topic: [cognitive_science/short/free_text_answer] Explore the neural and cognitive processes involved in metaphor comprehension. Examine how abstract metaphors recruit sensorimotor, linguistic, and executive
full_prompt: Explore the neural and cognitive processes involved in metaphor comprehension. Examine how abstract metaphors recruit sensorimotor, linguistic, and executive systems.
completion:
I will first locate recent and foundational neurocognitive work that triangulates metaphor comprehension across embodied semantics, language networks, and executive control.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "neural cognitive processes metaphor comprehension sensorimotor linguistic executive systems abstract metaphors fMRI meta-analysis", "year_min": 2008, "year_max": 2024, "k": 5}
```
[result]: The search returned key candidates including Desai et al. on the “neural career” of sensory-motor metaphors, Lacey et al. on somatosensory activation for texture metaphors, and Bohrn et al.’s quantitative meta-analysis of figurative language. Candidate OpenAlex IDs include W2147992547, W2078165214, and W2135427683.

I will read the meta-analysis because it can anchor a proposal that integrates distributed evidence across right/left language regions, sensory systems, and control networks.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2135427683"}
```
[result]: Bohrn et al. synthesize neuroimaging studies of figurative language and report reliable recruitment of bilateral inferior frontal regions, temporal language areas, and additional right-hemisphere and control-related regions depending on task difficulty, novelty, and semantic opacity. The paper highlights that figurative comprehension is not localized to a single “metaphor module” but varies with conventionality, familiarity, and interpretive demands.

These findings suggest a decisive research direction: test abstract metaphor comprehension as a dynamic negotiation among embodied feature simulation, linguistic-semantic integration, and executive selection rather than as either purely embodied or purely amodal.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "I propose a multimodal neurocognitive study of abstract metaphor comprehension that examines how sensorimotor, linguistic, and executive-control systems are differentially recruited as metaphors vary in abstractness, conventionality, and interpretive ambiguity. The central hypothesis is that even highly abstract metaphors can partially reactivate sensorimotor systems when their source domains contain stable embodied structure, but that this activation is gated by linguistic context and executive demands. For example, metaphors such as “grasp an idea,” “a rough negotiation,” and “a bright future” should differ in the degree to which motor, tactile, or visual systems contribute to comprehension, while all should depend on temporal and inferior frontal language regions for semantic integration.\n\nThe study would combine fMRI with behavioral measures and representational modeling. Participants would read literal sentences, conventional metaphors, novel metaphors, and abstract non-metaphorical controls while making meaning-related judgments. Stimuli would be normed for familiarity, imageability, emotional valence, source-domain concreteness, and comprehension difficulty. Neural analyses would test whether sensorimotor regions encode source-domain features, whether left temporal and inferior frontal regions track compositional semantic integration, and whether domain-general control regions such as dorsolateral prefrontal cortex and anterior cingulate are especially engaged by novel or ambiguous metaphors.\n\nA key innovation would be to model metaphor comprehension as a temporal and interactive process rather than a fixed localization problem. Representational similarity analyses could compare neural patterns with embeddings from language models, human feature norms, and sensorimotor ratings. If abstract metaphors recruit sensorimotor systems only when source-domain features are behaviorally relevant, this would support a flexible embodied account. If sensorimotor activation is absent but linguistic and executive regions explain comprehension, this would support a more symbolic or distributional account for highly abstract metaphor. A mixed outcome is most likely: embodied simulation may contribute early candidate meanings, while linguistic and executive systems select, suppress, and integrate interpretations in context.\n\nThis project would clarify why prior studies sometimes disagree about embodiment in metaphor comprehension. Rather than asking whether metaphors are embodied or linguistic, it asks when and how embodied, lexical-semantic, and executive mechanisms cooperate. The expected contribution is a mechanistic account in which abstract metaphor comprehension depends on graded sensorimotor grounding, contextual semantic integration, and cognitive control, with their relative contributions determined by metaphor novelty, source-domain concreteness, and task demands."}
```

topic: [cognitive_science/medium/gene_genome] Investigate how sleep affects insight problem solving and creative cognition. Compare performance after naps, overnight sleep, and equivalent periods of wakeful
full_prompt: Investigate how sleep affects insight problem solving and creative cognition. Compare performance after naps, overnight sleep, and equivalent periods of wakefulness.
completion:
I’ll first locate the canonical experimental papers that directly compare sleep intervals with matched wake intervals for insight/creativity tasks. This should separate overnight sleep effects from nap-specific REM/NREM effects.  
```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "sleep insight problem solving creative cognition nap overnight wakefulness REM number reduction task remote associates", "year_min": 2000, "year_max": 2024, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: Found core candidates: Wagner et al. “Sleep inspires insight” in *Nature* (OpenAlex: W1990490903; DOI 10.1038/nature02223), Cai et al. “REM, not incubation, improves creativity by priming associative networks” in *PNAS* (OpenAlex: W2102030817; DOI 10.1073/pnas.0900271106), and several later reviews/meta-analyses on sleep-dependent creativity and problem solving. Wagner gives an overnight sleep vs wake comparison; Cai gives nap REM/NREM vs quiet rest comparisons.  

To anchor the overnight component, I’ll read Wagner et al., because it operationalizes “insight” as discovery of a hidden shortcut after sleep or wake retention intervals.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W1990490903"}
```  
[result]: Wagner et al. trained participants on a number-reduction task containing a hidden rule, then retested them after either nocturnal sleep, nocturnal wakefulness, or daytime wakefulness. A substantially larger fraction of sleepers discovered the hidden rule, suggesting sleep restructures recently encoded task representations rather than merely reducing fatigue.  

Next I’ll read the nap study, since it distinguishes REM sleep from non-REM sleep and from an equivalent period of wake/rest for creative associative cognition.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2102030817"}
```  
[result]: Cai et al. used a Remote Associates Test design with morning exposure and afternoon retest after REM nap, NREM nap, or quiet rest. REM naps selectively improved performance on previously exposed problems relative to NREM and rest, consistent with REM-mediated associative priming rather than simple incubation or elapsed time.  

I’ll extract the causal “genome” from the nap paper to identify what mechanism, niche, observation, and limitation should carry into a new proposal.  
```action
{"tool": "extract_genome", "paper_id": "W2102030817"}
```  
[result]: Extracted pattern: mechanism = REM sleep broadens weak associative activation and integrates prior cues; niche = semantic creative problem solving after partial exposure; observation = REM nap improves recombination of primed associates more than NREM/rest; limitation = task-specific and hard to separate REM neurophysiology from nap timing, motivation, and sleep inertia.  

Before proposing, I’ll check whether a mechanistic comparison explicitly tying overnight NREM-rich abstraction and nap REM associative priming into one design has already become standard.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "sleep-stage-specific effects on insight: overnight NREM supports rule abstraction while nap REM supports weak associative recombination, compared with matched wake intervals", "year_min": 2010, "year_max": 2024}
```  
[result]: Later literature often treats sleep and creativity broadly, but direct within-framework comparisons of overnight sleep, REM naps, NREM naps, and matched wake intervals across both hidden-rule insight and semantic association tasks remain relatively sparse. The proposed angle is moderately novel if framed as a dissociation between abstraction-dominant and association-dominant creative mechanisms.  

I’ll now propose a compact gene-genome that synthesizes the evidence into a testable cognitive-science claim comparing naps, overnight sleep, and equivalent wakefulness.  
```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Sleep improves creative cognition through stage-sensitive representational restructuring: overnight sleep, especially NREM-rich slow-wave/spindle dynamics, promotes abstraction of latent task rules, while REM-rich naps promote weak semantic association and recombination of previously primed material.", "niche_genome": "Insight problem solving and creative cognition tasks where participants receive prior exposure or training, then undergo a controlled retention interval: overnight sleep, nocturnal/daytime wakefulness, REM nap, NREM nap, or quiet-rest wake control.", "observation_genome": "In the number-reduction task, overnight sleep yields higher discovery of a hidden shortcut than equivalent wake intervals; in Remote Associates-style tasks, REM naps improve creative recombination of previously exposed items more than NREM naps or quiet rest. Wakefulness can provide incubation time but is less reliable when the solution requires restructuring or weak-associate integration.", "limitation_genome": "Existing evidence relies on different tasks, different retention intervals, and sometimes imperfectly matched circadian timing, fatigue, sleep inertia, and expectancy. Nap studies isolate REM/NREM better than overnight studies, but overnight sleep combines multiple stages, making the precise stage-to-mechanism mapping underdetermined.", "delta_genome": "Run a within-subject crossover comparing the same participants on hidden-rule insight and semantic-association problems after four matched intervals: full overnight sleep, overnight wakefulness, REM-targeted nap, and quiet wake/rest, with polysomnography, circadian controls, and preregistered mediation by spindle density, slow-wave activity, and REM duration.", "claim_genome": "Sleep does not uniformly enhance creativity; it selectively benefits the form of creative cognition whose representational demand matches the dominant sleep physiology of the interval—overnight sleep favors latent-rule insight, whereas REM naps favor associative recombination—both outperforming matched wakefulness primarily when prior encoding supplies material for restructuring."}}
```  
[result]: Proposal generated as a stage-sensitive account of sleep-dependent insight and creativity, with a concrete comparative design spanning naps, overnight sleep, and wake controls.

topic: [cognitive_science/medium/idea_plan] Explore how bilingualism influences executive control across the lifespan. Compare children, young adults, and older adults on task-switching and inhibition mea
full_prompt: Explore how bilingualism influences executive control across the lifespan. Compare children, young adults, and older adults on task-switching and inhibition measures.
completion:
I’ll first map the key empirical literature that directly compares bilingualism with executive-control measures, prioritizing lifespan or age-stratified designs and tasks relevant to switching and inhibition.

{"tool": "search", "query": "bilingualism executive control lifespan children young adults older adults task switching inhibition Simon Flanker", "year_min": 2000, "year_max": 2024, "k": 5}
```  
[result]: Found several relevant candidates, including Bialystok et al. on bilingualism and cognitive control in aging, Barac & Bialystok on bilingual children’s executive control, and Paap & Greenberg on task-switching/Simon evidence in young adults. Candidate OpenAlex IDs include https://openalex.org/W2147083814, https://openalex.org/W2040527352, and https://openalex.org/W2163457951.
To anchor the child comparison, I’ll read a developmental study that separates bilingual status from plausible confounds such as culture and schooling while using executive-control outcomes.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W2040527352"}
```  
[result]: The paper reports that bilingual children often show advantages on nonverbal executive-control tasks, especially when tasks require managing conflict or selectively attending to goal-relevant dimensions. It emphasizes that bilingual effects are not uniform and depend on language background, socioeconomic/cultural factors, and task demands.
Next I’ll read a classic older-adult study because the proposed project needs to distinguish whether bilingualism has larger effects under age-related executive-control decline.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W2147083814"}
```  
[result]: This study found that bilingual older adults showed reduced age-related costs on the Simon task relative to monolinguals, suggesting preserved conflict monitoring or inhibitory control. The results were interpreted as evidence that lifelong bilingual language management may support executive control in aging, though the task focused more on interference/inhibition than explicit switching.
Because bilingual advantages in young adults are often smaller and more controversial, I’ll read a paper centered on switching and inhibition evidence in that age group.  
```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "https://openalex.org/W2163457951"}
```  
[result]: The paper argues that bilingual advantages in young adults are inconsistent across task-switching, Simon, and flanker measures, with many effects attenuating after accounting for publication bias, sample characteristics, and task reliability. It highlights the need for preregistered age-comparative designs with multiple indicators rather than single-task conclusions.
I’ll now check whether the core mechanism—a lifespan bilingualism effect tested with parallel switching and inhibition latent factors across children, young adults, and older adults—appears already saturated or whether a sharper design contribution remains.  
```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "lifespan bilingualism comparison using parallel task-switching and inhibition measures with age group by bilingual language-experience interactions and latent executive-control factors", "year_min": 2015, "year_max": 2024}
```  
[result]: Recent work contains many bilingual executive-control studies, but fewer directly compare children, young adults, and older adults in one harmonized design using both switching and inhibition tasks and continuous bilingual-experience variables. A novel contribution would be a preregistered, multi-task, age-stratified study testing whether bilingualism predicts executive control differently across developmental gains, young-adult plateau, and older-adult decline.
Based on the readings, the strongest design is not another single-task “bilingual advantage” test, but a harmonized lifespan study that models bilingualism continuously and compares switching versus inhibition across age groups.  
```action

full_prompt: Research how cities can redesign streets to reduce traffic fatalities under Vision Zero policies. Compare the effectiveness of speed reduction, protected intersections, road diets, and automated enforcement.
completion:
I’ll first scan for empirical and review evidence spanning the four intervention families: lower speeds, protected intersections/bike-protected designs, road diets, and automated enforcement under Vision Zero-style safety goals.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "Vision Zero urban street redesign speed reduction protected intersections road diets automated enforcement traffic fatalities crash reduction", "year_min": 2000, "year_max": 2024, "k": 5}
```

[result]: Candidate papers included Grundy et al. on London 20 mph zones, Wilson et al. on speed cameras, Huang/Stewart/Zegeer on lane-reduction road diets, and review evidence on protected bicycle facilities/intersections. OpenAlex candidates returned included `W2050234116`, `W2149220766`, `W2035352389`, `W2083938508`, and `W2165873524`.

The speed-management evidence is central because Vision Zero usually treats kinetic energy reduction as the core fatality-prevention mechanism.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2050234116"}
```

[result]: Grundy et al. found that 20 mph zones in London were associated with sizable reductions in road casualties, especially among children and vulnerable road users. The mechanism appears to be both lower impact speed and traffic-calming-induced reductions in vehicle dominance, though the study is strongest for area-based zones rather than isolated posted-limit changes.

Next I’ll read automated enforcement evidence, since it can reinforce speed reduction without requiring every street to be fully reconstructed.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2149220766"}
```

[result]: The speed-camera review found consistent crash and injury reductions near camera sites and within enforced corridors, with stronger effects when enforcement was visible, sustained, and targeted to high-risk locations. Evidence supports automated enforcement as a compliance tool, but equity, siting transparency, and revenue concerns are recurring implementation constraints.

Road diets are a design intervention that can reduce speed and conflict exposure while reallocating space to pedestrians, cyclists, and transit.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2035352389"}
```

[result]: The road-diet evaluation literature reports crash reductions after converting four-lane undivided roads to three-lane cross-sections with a center turn lane, especially on moderate-volume arterials with speeding or turning-conflict problems. Benefits are context-sensitive: designs work best where traffic volumes remain within operational capacity and where pedestrian/bicycle crossings are also upgraded.

To position the proposal, I’ll check whether the combined mechanism—self-enforcing street geometry plus automated speed compliance plus protected conflict-point design—is already treated as a unified Vision Zero evaluation frame.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "Vision Zero street redesign package combining speed reduction, road diets, protected intersections, and automated enforcement with intervention-specific effectiveness comparison", "year_min": 2015, "year_max": 2024}
```

[result]: Prior work often evaluates these interventions separately: speed zones/cameras as enforcement or policy tools, road diets as corridor redesign, and protected intersections as bicycle/pedestrian conflict treatments. Fewer studies frame them as a layered fatality-prevention system with different causal roles—energy reduction, conflict separation, exposure reduction, and compliance maintenance.

I’ll propose a synthesis genome that compares effectiveness while identifying where the evidence is strongest and where cities should be cautious.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "gene_genome": {"mechanism_genome": "Cities reduce fatalities most effectively when street redesign lowers crash energy and removes high-conflict interactions: default lower speeds reduce impact severity; road diets narrow and simplify vehicle movements; protected intersections separate turning vehicles from pedestrians/cyclists; automated enforcement sustains compliance where design alone is insufficient.", "niche_genome": "Vision Zero implementation on urban arterials, high-injury networks, school zones, downtown corridors, and neighborhood main streets where pedestrians and cyclists mix with motor traffic and where fatal/severe crashes cluster.", "observation_genome": "Evidence is strongest for speed reduction and automated speed enforcement: 20 mph/30 km/h zones and speed cameras repeatedly show casualty reductions. Road diets also show meaningful crash reductions on suitable four-lane undivided roads, particularly by reducing speeding, weaving, and left-turn conflicts. Protected intersections are theoretically strong and increasingly adopted, but direct fatality-reduction evidence is thinner because installations are newer and severe crashes are statistically rare.", "limitation_genome": "Posted speed limits without geometric change may have weak effects; automated enforcement can face political, equity, and privacy objections; road diets can fail or create diversion if applied on over-capacity corridors without network planning; protected intersections require careful signal timing, visibility, maintenance, and driver yielding behavior. Cross-study comparisons are difficult because interventions are often bundled and outcomes vary between total crashes, injury crashes, severe injuries, and fatalities.", "delta_genome": "Instead of ranking interventions as substitutes, evaluate them as complementary layers: use road diets and protected intersections to create self-explaining, forgiving streets; use lower statutory speeds to set the safety target; use automated enforcement to close the compliance gap at high-risk corridors and times. Prioritize locations through a high-injury-network screen and measure severe/fatal injury change, operating speed distributions, yielding behavior, and equity impacts.", "claim_genome": "For Vision Zero, the most defensible strategy is not a single intervention but a package: speed reduction is the core fatality mechanism, automated enforcement is the strongest compliance amplifier, road diets are effective corridor-level redesigns where volumes fit, and protected intersections are essential conflict-point treatments for vulnerable users despite a less mature fatality-specific evidence base."}}
```

However, the interaction is typically non-interactive: participants see others’ judgments but do not negotiate, justify, coordinate, or make a shared decision in real time.  

Because the user asks specifically about group discussion or cooperative tasks, I next read a paper from the collective-decision literature rather than another vignette-based moral study. This helps import mechanisms for how interaction changes judgments beyond simple conformity.

It suggests a useful design principle: compare private pre-judgments, live joint deliberation, and private post-judgments to separate evidence pooling from social pressure.

Existing work covers moral conformity, group deliberation, or cooperative decision-making separately, but fewer studies jointly model moment-by-moment interaction traces, private moral updating, and the distinction between group consensus and individual internalization.

topic: [cognitive_science/short/free_text_answer] Study how humans learn from explanation compared with trial-and-error feedback. Test whether self-generated explanations improve transfer to novel problems.
full_prompt: Study how humans learn from explanation compared with trial-and-error feedback. Test whether self-generated explanations improve transfer to novel problems.
completion:
I’ll first locate the closest cognitive-science literature linking self-explanation, feedback-based learning, and transfer, so the proposal can be anchored in known effects rather than reinventing the paradigm.
I’ll first locate the closest cognitive-science literature linking self-explanation, feedback-based learning, and transfer, so the proposal can be anchored in known effects rather than reinventing the paradigm.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "self-generated explanations trial and error feedback learning transfer novel problems cognitive science self-explanation effect", "year_min": 1988, "year_max": 2024, "k": 5}
```

[result]: The search returns classic and recent work on the self-explanation effect, including Chi et al.’s “Self-explanations: How students study and use examples in learning to solve problems” and later studies showing that prompted explanations improve conceptual transfer more than passive study. It also surfaces reinforcement/feedback-learning studies where learners improve on trained items but often show weaker far transfer without abstraction support.

I’ll use the search result directly rather than over-reading, because the core empirical gap is clear: explanation and trial-and-error feedback are often studied separately, but not always compared under matched exposure with transfer as the primary outcome. 

```action
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "This project will test whether humans learn more transferable problem-solving knowledge from self-generated explanations than from trial-and-error feedback alone. The central hypothesis is that feedback improves performance on practiced problems by strengthening action–outcome associations, whereas self-explanation promotes abstraction of underlying principles and therefore improves transfer to novel problems. A secondary hypothesis is that combining feedback with self-explanation will yield the strongest transfer, because learners can use outcome information to revise and articulate causal rules.\n\nParticipants will learn a small artificial problem domain, such as symbolic balance-scale problems, logic-grid transformations, or unfamiliar category-structure puzzles, where surface features can be varied independently from deep rules. They will be randomly assigned to one of three conditions: trial-and-error feedback, in which they solve each training item and receive correctness feedback; self-explanation, in which they solve or inspect items and must explain why the correct answer follows; and combined feedback-plus-explanation, in which they receive feedback and then generate an explanation. Exposure time, number of examples, and response opportunities will be matched as closely as possible across conditions.\n\nThe main dependent measure will be transfer to novel problems that preserve the same deep structure but alter surface features, formats, or required intermediate steps. The study will distinguish near transfer, involving familiar-looking items with new values, from far transfer, involving new cover stories or representational formats. Training accuracy and response times will assess immediate learning, while explanation quality will be coded for references to deep rules, surface features, and causal/mechanistic relations. The critical prediction is that trial-and-error feedback will produce comparable or better training performance, but self-explanation—especially high-quality self-explanation—will produce superior far transfer.\n\nThis design would clarify whether the advantage of explanation lies in better initial learning, better abstraction, or both. If self-generated explanations improve transfer even when training accuracy is equated, that would support the view that explanation changes the representation learners form, not merely the amount they learn. The findings would have practical implications for education and intelligent tutoring systems: instead of only telling learners whether they are correct, systems may need to elicit explanations at moments when learners can connect feedback to generalizable principles."}
```

Next I’ll read the mathematics-learning study to see whether gesture only lightens cognitive load during solving or can restructure the solver’s strategy space.

```action

It remains unclear when gesture merely supports current performance versus when it causes durable conceptual change.", "delta_genome": "Test a factorial design crossing gesture encouragement, non-gestural external diagrams/manipulatives, and working-memory load, with immediate accuracy, gesture-speech mismatch, strategy coding, and delayed transfer as outcomes. The key discriminant is that pure offloading predicts immediate gains under load, whereas restructuring predicts novel strategies and delayed transfer even after gesture is no longer allowed.", "claim_genome": "Encouraging gesture facilitates problem solving not simply by reducing cognitive load and not simply by expressing hidden knowledge, but by creating an embodied representational workspace: it can offload spatial computation in the moment and, when aligned with deep task structure, reorganize the learner’s problem representation for later mathematical and spatial reasoning."}}

topic: [philosophy_ethics/medium/free_text_answer] Analyze the ethical implications of artificial intelligence systems making decisions in healthcare triage. What moral framework best addresses accountabilit
full_prompt: Analyze the ethical implications of artificial intelligence systems making decisions in healthcare triage. What moral framework best addresses accountability when outcomes are harmful?
completion:
To ground the ethical analysis, I first look for recent philosophy/medical-ethics literature on AI in healthcare decision-making, with emphasis on triage, bias, explainability, and accountability.
I’ll ground the analysis in recent medical-AI ethics literature, then synthesize which moral framework best handles harmful triage outcomes.

```action
{"tool": "search", "query": "artificial intelligence healthcare triage ethics accountability harmful outcomes moral responsibility", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2024, "k": 5}
```

[result]: Found relevant work including Char, Shah, and Magnus on ethical challenges in ML implementation in healthcare; Morley et al. on AI health-care ethics; Obermeyer et al. on racial bias in health algorithms; and London on black-box medical decisions. Candidate OpenAlex IDs included W2798536294, W3046013954, W2973680428, and W2963346317.

A central issue is whether accountability can be assigned when AI systems are embedded in clinical workflows rather than acting as isolated “agents.” I read a broad ethics review to identify recurring moral concerns and accountability gaps.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W3046013954"}
```

[result]: The review maps major ethical themes in healthcare AI: transparency, fairness, privacy, trust, safety, responsibility, and governance. It emphasizes that accountability is distributed across developers, hospitals, clinicians, regulators, and procurers, making simple blame-based models inadequate.

To sharpen the triage problem, I next examine empirical evidence that algorithmic health decisions can cause systematic harm, especially when proxies or training data encode inequity. This matters because harmful triage outcomes are often not random errors but foreseeable institutional risks.

```action
{"tool": "read", "paper_id": "W2973680428"}
```

[result]: Obermeyer et al. show that a widely used health-risk algorithm exhibited racial bias because it used healthcare cost as a proxy for medical need. The study illustrates how apparently neutral optimization can misallocate care, making accountability depend on design choices, validation procedures, and institutional adoption.

The emerging hypothesis is that neither pure consequentialism nor individual clinical deontology alone fully captures the accountability problem. I check whether a hybrid “relational responsibility” approach—combining procedural justice, non-delegable professional duties, and forward-looking institutional accountability—is a distinctive way to frame triage ethics.

```action
{"tool": "novelty_check", "mechanism": "relational responsibility framework for AI healthcare triage combining non-delegable clinical duty, procedural justice, auditability, and forward-looking institutional accountability", "year_min": 2018, "year_max": 2024}
```

[result]: Similar themes appear across AI ethics, responsible innovation, and medical professionalism, but the combined framing is relatively underdeveloped for emergency triage specifically. The most promising contribution is to treat accountability as layered: answerability before deployment, contestability during use, and reparative responsibility after harm.

```action
{"tool": "propose", "answer": "Artificial intelligence in healthcare triage raises a distinctive ethical problem because triage is not merely predictive; it is distributive. A triage system helps decide who receives scarce attention, beds, diagnostic priority, or life-saving intervention. Even when the system is statistically accurate overall, harmful outcomes may fall disproportionately on already disadvantaged patients if the model relies on biased data, inappropriate proxies, or institutional patterns of unequal access. The moral issue is therefore not only whether the algorithm performs well, but whether its use preserves equal moral regard for patients under conditions of scarcity.\n\nThe main ethical risks are opacity, bias, de-skilling, and responsibility gaps. If clinicians cannot understand or challenge an AI recommendation, patients may be denied meaningful explanation or appeal. If hospitals treat the system as authoritative, clinicians may defer to it even when contextual judgment is required. If developers blame clinicians, clinicians blame software, and institutions blame vendors, then harmful outcomes become morally orphaned. This is especially troubling in triage because delay or exclusion can directly cause death, disability, or preventable suffering.\n\nThe moral framework that best addresses accountability is a relational and institutional responsibility framework, rather than a purely consequentialist or purely individualist one. Consequentialism is useful because triage must consider outcomes and population-level benefit, but it can excuse severe injustice if aggregate efficiency improves. A narrow deontological model rightly insists that each patient must be treated as a person rather than as a data point, but it may understate the collective nature of AI design, procurement, monitoring, and governance. A relational responsibility approach combines both insights: it asks whether the system produces beneficial outcomes, whether it respects each patient’s dignity and claim to fair consideration, and whether identifiable human institutions remain answerable for its use.\n\nUnder this framework, accountability is non-delegable. Developers are responsible for data quality, validation, bias testing, and communicating limitations. Hospitals are responsible for procurement, local testing, workflow design, monitoring, and ensuring that staff can override or contest recommendations. Clinicians retain professional responsibility not to follow AI outputs blindly, though they should not be scapegoated for systemic failures beyond their control. Regulators and public institutions are responsible for setting standards of transparency, auditability, safety, and redress. When harm occurs, the central question should not be simply “Which individual is to blame?” but “Which agents had power to foresee, prevent, detect, or repair this failure?”\n\nThus, the best ethical answer is a layered accountability model grounded in procedural justice, professional duty, and forward-looking institutional responsibility. AI triage can be morally permissible only if patients are assessed according to clinically relevant need, systems are auditable for bias and error, decisions remain contestable, and harms trigger correction and compensation rather than denial or blame-shifting. In healthcare triage, accountability must follow power: those who design, deploy, authorize, and rely on AI systems must remain morally and legally responsible for the consequences of their use."} 
```
